does: This paper applies sparse autoencoders to decompose the learned representations of a ConvNeXt vision model trained on aircraft classification. The authors extract spatial activations from the model's final feature layer, train a sparse autoencoder to factorize these activations into interpretable components, and analyze the resulting features through qualitative inspection, activation statistics, and class selectivity metrics. They then validate feature importance through ablation experiments that measure how suppressing individual sparse features or blurring corresponding image patches affects model predictions, logits, and confidence scores. The findings demonstrate that sparse autoencoders can recover semantically meaningful, class-relevant features corresponding to recognizable aircraft structures and visual patterns.
connects: This work builds on recent efforts to interpret deep neural networks by decomposing learned representations into human-understandable components, extending prior research on mechanistic interpretability and feature attribution in vision models. It draws on the sparse autoencoder framework developed for language models, adapting it to the convolutional feature space of vision models. The paper connects to broader work on concept-based interpretability and fine-grained visual classification, addressing the general challenge that vision models achieve strong performance without transparent decision boundaries. The focus on aircraft classification continues a tradition of analyzing models on specialized domains where ground-truth visual features are well-understood.
enables: This study opens pathways for scaling interpretability methods to larger and more complex vision models by validating sparse autoencoders as an unsupervised approach to feature discovery. The demonstrated ability to recover semantically meaningful aircraft-specific features suggests potential applications to other fine-grained classification domains where interpretability is critical. The work points toward automated methods for identifying decision-relevant visual concepts without manual annotation, which could improve model debugging, bias detection, and trustworthiness in safety-critical applications. Future work could extend this framework to multi-layer analysis, evaluate the completeness of discovered feature sets, and develop better metrics for quantifying interpretability gains across different model architectures and datasets.
